lesson_contract.objective: Learn the BMH Group service standard and the mindset that keeps seller conversations clear, calm, and human.
objective_points: Use curiosity and clarity in seller conversations
Detach from outcomes while staying accountable
lesson_contract.transition_spoken_text: Take the Welcome and Mindset checkpoint next. Then move into Terms Glossary.

required_teaching_beats.description: Help even when BMH is not the right solution.
Understand the seller's burden before discussing property data.
Help the seller picture a workable result and path.
Own preparation, tone, notes, and follow-through.
Ask a simple question and leave room for the answer.
Speak peer to peer without pleading or lecturing.
Follow up usefully as the seller's situation changes.
Review work, practice, and apply a correction.
Restate the concern accurately before proposing a next step.
The ten principles work together as a conversation mindset.
The close directs the learner to the checkpoint and Terms Glossary.

required_examples.description: An inherited property far from the seller shows service over self.
A tired landlord shows why the burden matters before property data.
A seller who needs to think may be missing a visible path.
An unanswered contact separates controllable actions from outcomes.
Leaving silence gives the seller room to reveal the useful detail.
A number that does not fit can be discussed directly and respectfully.
A seller's situation can change without following a fixed timeline.
A money objection becomes clearer when its purpose is understood.
Reviewing a rushed moment turns practice into a correction.
Restating a concern prevents an argument over the wrong issue.

forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Use decision-stage language with no fixed seller progression.
Use decision-stage language with no fixed seller progression.

scenes.title: How you enter the conversation
scenes.spoken_text: Before you begin a seller conversation, settle your attention. You are there to understand the situation, make the available next step clearer, and leave an accurate record. The words matter, but the mindset underneath them will shape how those words land. These ten principles give you something steady to return to when a conversation moves away from the script or starts to feel tense.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea in a calm medium shot
visual_plan.editor_note: Open with a quiet reset before the principles begin.

scenes.title: Service over self
scenes.spoken_text: First, start with service. Imagine someone inherited a house far from home. They may care less about squeezing out the last possible dollar than finding a path they can understand and manage. Ask what would make the situation easier. If BMH is not the right fit, say that plainly and help the person identify a responsible next step. The conversation can still be useful even when it does not become a deal.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a seller looking at a distant property on a simple map
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no named market or fixed travel distance.

scenes.title: Get on the seller's side of the table
scenes.spoken_text: Second, understand the burden before you study the property. A tired landlord may mention a roof, a tenant, or a repair bill, while the deeper issue is that the property has been consuming attention for too long. Say back what you heard. You might say, It sounds like you want your time back and you need a path that does not create another project. That tells the seller you are listening to the life around the house.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea listening to a seller beside a worn property folder
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the emphasis on the seller's burden rather than the repair list.

scenes.title: Clarity before pressure
scenes.spoken_text: Third, help the seller see the path. When someone says they need to think, the decision may still feel blurry. Ask what part is unresolved. Where would they live next? Which responsibility worries them? What would they need to verify before a decision felt responsible? Do not push for an answer while the picture is incomplete. Turn the vague hesitation into a specific question the seller can examine.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a seller sorting a few unlabeled decision cards
visual_plan.editor_note: The path becomes clearer without a countdown or urgency device.

scenes.title: Own the actions you control
scenes.spoken_text: Fourth, separate your work from the seller's choice. Someone may not answer, may choose another path, or may change their mind. You still control your preparation, your tone, the accuracy of your notes, and whether you complete the follow-up you promised. If a contact goes unanswered, leave the record ready for the next attempt and move to the next assigned action. Accountability stays useful even when the outcome does not cooperate.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a simple preparation, notes, and follow-through loop
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no call count, quota, or results chart.

scenes.title: Curiosity over cleverness
scenes.spoken_text: Fifth, ask a simple question and wait. Suppose the seller says, The house has become too much. You could jump into a solution, or you could ask, What part has become hardest to manage? Then leave enough silence for the real answer. The seller may reveal a repair, a family disagreement, or an approaching move. You did not need a clever line. You needed enough curiosity to hear the detail that changes the conversation.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea seated with open listening posture and an uncluttered speech bubble
visual_plan.editor_note: Leave a natural beat after the question.

scenes.title: Use equal status
scenes.spoken_text: Sixth, speak peer to peer. You do not need to beg for agreement, and you do not need to lecture. If a number does not fit the current written process, say that respectfully. Ask how the seller arrived at it and what the money needs to accomplish. Direct language can stay warm. The seller should feel that you are taking the concern seriously without pretending that every request is available.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea in an even eye-level conversational frame
visual_plan.editor_note: Avoid dominant or pleading body language.

scenes.title: Let decisions mature
scenes.spoken_text: Seventh, respect the seller's decision stage. A person who is not ready may become ready after a repair estimate arrives, another option falls through, or the family reaches agreement. Follow up with a useful reason. Bring new information when you have it. Ask what changed instead of replaying the same pitch. There is no fixed progression to memorize. The facts in the seller's life tell you whether the conversation has moved.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a seller whose situation cards change
visual_plan.editor_note: Show changing facts without a calendar, clock, or fixed stage timeline.

scenes.title: Treat objections as missing information
scenes.spoken_text: Eighth, look for the missing piece inside the objection. If someone says they need more money, ask what that money needs to cover. It may be a loan balance, moving costs, another commitment, or simply an expectation they have not explained yet. Once you know the purpose, you can tell whether the current process has a responsible option or whether the gap remains. The objection becomes a fact-finding problem instead of a wall.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a simple missing-piece diagram and property folder
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no currency figures or promise language.

scenes.title: Practice creates calm
scenes.spoken_text: Ninth, use repetition to become steadier. Review a conversation when the current process allows it. Find the place where you rushed, filled the silence, or missed a useful question. Choose a small correction and practice it out loud. In the next conversation, focus on that correction rather than trying to sound perfect. Calm grows when you have heard the situation before and know what you want to do differently.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea reviewing a generic conversation note and practicing a calmer response
visual_plan.editor_note: Show reflection and retry without a score or activity counter.

scenes.title: Understand before you argue
scenes.spoken_text: Tenth, check that you understand before you defend a position. A seller may say the offer feels unfair when the deeper concern is that a family member will question the decision. Restate the concern: It sounds like you need everyone involved to understand how the terms were reached. Then ask if you have it right. That short check can keep you from arguing about price when the real need is shared understanding.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea in a steady medium shot with a simple listen, restate, check sequence
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the sequence conversational rather than instructional on screen.

scenes.title: Continue to the checkpoint
scenes.spoken_text: Take the Welcome and Mindset checkpoint next. Then move into Terms Glossary.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea centered in a medium shot

edit_operations.source_problem: Fixed week progression is embedded in the long-game principle. A clean full narration keeps all ten principles and their examples while removing the timeline claim.
